title: Enforce Dedicated Config Servers for Sharded Clusters

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
- Ensure your Atlas user has the appropriate organization role:

  - To view Atlas Resource Policies, you must have at least [Organization Read Only](<Organization Read Only>) or
    [Organization Member](<Organization Member>) access to Atlas.

  - To create, update, or delete an Atlas Resource Policy, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>)
    access to Atlas.

- To use the Atlas Administration API to create or manage Atlas Resource Policies, locate your public and private API (Application Programming Interface) keys so you can authenticate to the
  [Atlas Administration API](atlas-admin-api). To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
- *We strongly recommend that you proactively monitor your list of non-compliant resources*
  using the [/orgs/\{ORG-ID}/nonCompliantResources](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getorgnoncompliantresources) endpoint.
  Atlas Resource Policies ensure compliance with defined goals but do not enforce intermediate
  or transitional states. For example, if a downstream component fails, the policy
  may temporarily enter a non-compliant intermediate state.

- Each Atlas Resource Policy must have a unique name. If you attempt to create an Atlas Resource Policy with
  an existing name, the server returns a `400 (Bad Request)` status code.

- It's possible to create identical Atlas Resource Policies with different names. While this doesn't
  cause functional conflicts, it can lead to redundancy and confusion in policy management.

- Atlas Resource Policies are allow-by-default. If no Atlas Resource Policies exist, users can perform all actions on Atlas clusters
  that their [Atlas User Roles](user-roles) allows.

- Our implemented version of [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/) supports only one Cedar policy per `.cedar` file, but you
  can list multiple Cedar policies (`.cedar` files) in your Atlas Resource Policy.
  Each `.cedar` file is identified in the [Atlas Administration API](atlas-admin-api)
  response as a unique 24-hexadecimal character string after `policies.id`.

- If the [Cluster Auto-Scaling](cluster-autoscaling) limits on your existing cluster
  don't match the new Atlas Resource Policy, the cluster might exceed the policy's defined boundaries.
  To address this issue, use the [/orgs/\{ORG-ID}/nonCompliantResources](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/orgs/\{ORG-ID}/nonCompliantResources)
  endpoint to identify and monitor non-compliant resources so you can update them to meet
  policy requirements.

- Before enabling network-layer controls, review your existing [IP Access List](access-list)
  to ensure it contains all necessary access points.

- Private endpoint definitions in [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/) require that you enter individual IP addresses,
  as wildcard operations are not supported.

- When enforcing disk size, ensure you account for the specific requirements of your
  instance sizes and cloud provider limits.

- If you [Enforce Minimum Shard Count](restrict-shard-count) greater than one,
  you must explicitly allow the transition state for cluster conversions in your policy
  (for example, `&& !context.cluster.isConvertingToSharded`).
  Without this exception, Atlas blocks users from converting a replica set to
  a sharded cluster because the conversion process requires an intermediate state
  where the cluster has only one shard.

- Atlas treats a replica set as having a shard count of one. If you enforce a
  minimum shard count greater than one without checking whether clusters are sharded clusters,
  all replica sets in the project are marked as non-compliant.

sections.heading: Using the Cedar Policy Language
sections.body:
MongoDB uses the open-source [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/) to define Atlas Resource Policies.
Cedar's design balances expressiveness with simplicity, employing a concise syntax
that streamlines both writing and understanding resource policies.

To quickly create an Atlas Resource Policy, adapt the [examples](arp-examples) and add to Atlas
using the Atlas UI or Atlas Administration API by following [Create an Atlas Resource Policy Configuration](procedure-create-atlas-resource-policy).

To learn more about [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/), see:

- [Key Cedar Syntax Elements for Atlas](key-cedar-elements)

- [Restrict Cloud Provider and Region](example-cedar-policy)

- [Cedar GitHub Repository](https://github.com/cedar-policy).

sections.heading: Key Cedar Syntax Elements for Atlas
sections.body:
| Cedar Element                                    | Descripton                                                                                                                                                      | Atlas Resource Policy Options                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| ------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| forbid                                           | Denies the specified action if conditions in the when clause are met. The converse element, permit, is not allowed in Atlas Resource Policies.                  | Example: forbid (principal, action, resource) when \{ ... };                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| principal                                        | Defines the user affected by the policy.                                                                                                                        | Applied universally by default. Do not change or omit.                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| action                                           | Represents the action being controlled in the policy.                                                                                                           | Available actions:Restrict IP access list: ResourcePolicy::Action::"project.ipAccessList.modify"Restrict cluster attributes: ResourcePolicy::Action::"cluster.modify"Modify maintenance window: ResourcePolicy::Action::"project.maintenanceWindow\.modify" |
| ResourcePolicy::Cluster::"\<CLUSTER-ID>"         | Refers to a specific cluster                                                                                                                                    | To find your cluster ID, use the Atlas Administration API to return all clusters in a project.                                                                                                                                                              |
| ResourcePolicy::Project::"\<PROJECT\_ID>"        | Refers to a specific project. For example, ResourcePolicy::Project::"6217f7fff7957854e2d09179"                                                                  | To find your project ID, see Manage Project Settings.                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| context.cluster.project                          | Specifies the project to which the cluster belongs.                                                                                                             | To find your project ID, see Manage Project Settings.                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| context.cluster.cloudProviders                   | Specifies the allowed cloud providers.                                                                                                                          | Available options:ResourcePolicy::CloudProvider::"aws"ResourcePolicy::CloudProvider::"azure"ResourcePolicy::CloudProvider::"gcp"                                                                                                                            |
| context.cluster.clusterType                      | Specifies the cluster topology: replica set, sharded cluster, or global cluster.                                                                                | Available options:ResourcePolicy::ClusterType::"replicaset"ResourcePolicy::ClusterType::"sharded"ResourcePolicy::ClusterType::"geosharded"                                                                                                                  |
| context.cluster.diskSizeGB                       | Specifies the disk size in GB.                                                                                                                                  | For example, context.cluster.diskSizeGB \< 100 matches clusters with less than 100 GB of storage.                                                                                                                                                           |
| context.cluster.minShardCount                    | The current actual number of shards. Use only to enforce minimum shard counts. WARNING: Do not use this property to enforce maximums.                           | For example, context.cluster.minShardCount \< 3 matches clusters that currently have fewer than three shards.                                                                                                                                               |
| context.cluster.maxShardCount                    | The maximum possible number of shards. Use only to enforce maximum shard limits. WARNING: Do not use this property to enforce minimums.                         | For example, context.cluster.maxShardCount > 10 matches clusters that have more than ten shards.                                                                                                                                                            |
| context.cluster.isConvertingToSharded            | Checks if the current operation is converting a replica set to a sharded cluster. Required when enforcing minimum shard counts to allow the initial transition. | For example, context.cluster.isConvertingToSharded == true                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| context.cluster.regions                          | Limits access based on deployment regions.                                                                                                                      | For example, ResourcePolicy::Region::"aws:us-east-1". For a list of available regions by cloud provider see Amazon Web Services (AWS), Google Cloud Platform (GCP), or Microsoft Azure.                                                                     |
| context.cluster has \<PROPERTY>                  | Checks if a specific property exists within the cluster context.                                                                                                | Available properties:Minimum General class cluster tier: minGeneralClassInstanceSizeValueMaximum General class cluster tier: maxGeneralClassInstanceSizeValueDisk size (GB): diskSizeGB                                                                     |
| context.cluster.minGeneralClassInstanceSizeValue | Sets the minimum cluster tier for scaling the cluster down to.                                                                                                  | For example, context.cluster.minGeneralClassInstanceSizeValue \< 30 matches clusters whose current tier or minimum auto-scaling size is less than M30.                                                                                                      |
| context.cluster.maxGeneralClassInstanceSizeValue | Sets the maximum cluster tier for scaling the cluster up to.                                                                                                    | For example, context.cluster.maxGeneralClassInstanceSizeValue \< 60 matches clusters whose current tier or maximum auto-scaling size is less than M60.                                                                                                      |
| context.project.clustersInProject                | Specifies the number of clusters currently in a project.                                                                                                        | For example, context.project.clustersInProject \<= 3 matches projects with three or fewer clusters.                                                                                                                                                         |
| context.project.hasDefinedMaintenanceWindow      | Requires that a project has a maintenance window configured.                                                                                                    | For example, context.project.hasDefinedMaintenanceWindow == false checks if the project has no maintenance window configured.                                                                                                                               |
| context.project.ipAccessList                     | Specifies the IP addresses that can access the cluster.                                                                                                         | You can block a wildcard IP ip("0.0.0.0/0") to enhance security.                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| context.project.peeringConnections               | Refers to a specific VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) peering connection.                                                                                            | To format for your specific cloud provider, see Prevent Modifications to Network Peering.                                                                                                                                                                   |
| context.project.privateEndpoints                 | Refers to a specific private endpoint.                                                                                                                          | To format for your specific cloud provider, see Prevent Modifications to Private Endpoints.                                                                                                                                                                 |
| containsAny and containsAll                      | Checks for inclusion of one or more elements (containsAny) or all elements (containsAll) from a list.                                                           | Example: context.cluster.regions.containsAny(\[ResourcePolicy::Region::"aws:us-east-1"])                                                                                                                                                                    |
| unless                                           | Specifies exceptions to a policy. Actions are forbidden unless certain conditions are met.                                                                      | Example: forbid (principal, action, resource) unless \{ condition };                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| isEmpty                                          | Checks whether a list is empty. For example, enforcing no access over public networks requires that the IP access list is empty.                                | forbid (principal, action == ResourcePolicy::Action::"project.ipAccessList.modify", resource) unless \{ context.project.ipAccessList.isEmpty() };                                                                                                           |
| Logical Operators (&&, \|\|, !)                  | Combine multiple conditions in a policy.                                                                                                                        | Available options:&& (AND): Requires all conditions to be true.\|\| (OR): Requires at least one condition to be true.! (NOT): Requires a condition to be false.                                                                                             |
| context.cluster.configServerManagementMode       | Checks for config server management mode on sharded clusters.                                                                                                   | Available options:ResourcePolicy::ConfigServerManagementMode::"fixed\_to\_dedicated" for dedicated config servers.ResourcePolicy::ConfigServerManagementMode::"atlas\_managed" for embedded config servers.                                                 |
| IP Addressing (ip)                               | Refers to specific IP addresses or ranges in policies.                                                                                                          | Available options:ip("0.0.0.0/0") for wildcard IP.ip("1.2.3.4/32") for specific IP addresses.                                                                                                                                                               |

sections.heading: Create an Atlas Resource Policy Configuration
sections.body:
To create an Atlas Resource Policy, use the following procedure to construct a policy by using [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/)
and add to Atlas by using the Atlas UI, the Atlas Administration API, or Terraform.> **Warning:** We recommend that you test this feature in a non-production environment by creating
> a new organization with a fresh cluster.
> This ensures you can assess the feature safely without impacting your existing systems as you skill up.**Api:**Atlas Administration APITo create an Atlas Resource Policy using the Atlas Administration API, construct a policy using
[Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/) and pass it in to the Atlas Administration API via a `POST` request
using the `policies` parameter.

sections.heading: Step 1: Construct the policy in the Cedar policy language.
sections.body:
Specify the rules for restricting resources using [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/). You can copy
and modify the following example policies for your organization:

- [Restrict Number of Clusters](restrict-number-of-clusters-api)

- [Restrict Cloud Provider](restrict-cloud-provider)

- [Restrict Cloud Provider Region](restrict-region)

- [Restrict Cloud Provider and Region](example-cedar-policy)

- [Restrict IP Addresses](restrict-ip-addresses)

- [Restrict Cluster Tier Sizes](restrict-cluster-tier)

- [Require Project Maintenance Windows](require-maintenance-window)

- [Prevent Modifications to Network Peering](prevent-peering-modifications)

- [Prevent Modifications to Private Endpoints](prevent-private-endpoint-modifications)

- [Restrict TLS (Transport Layer Security) Protocol and Cipher Suites](restrict-tls-api)

- [Enforce Disk Size (GB)](enforce-disk-size)

- [Enforce Cluster Topology (Replica Set, Sharded, or Global Cluster)](restrict-cluster-type)

- [Enforce Minimum Shard Count](restrict-shard-count)

- [Enforce Dedicated Config Servers for Sharded Clusters](enforce-config-server-management-mode)

> **Note:** We recommend creating multiple, simple Atlas Resource Policies to make tracking easier.
> For example, if you want to restrict cloud provider and multiple regions,
> consider creating one Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the cloud provider and another
> Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the regions.

sections.heading: Step 2: Send a  request.
Step 2: Name, describe, and define your Atlas Resource Policy.
sections.body:
- Give your Atlas Resource Policy a unique name.

- (Optional) Add a description.

- Copy and modify the following example policies for your organization,
  then paste the code in the **Cedar Policy** field.

  - [Restrict Number of Clusters](restrict-number-of-clusters-ui)

  - [Restrict Cloud Provider](restrict-cloud-provider-ui)

  - [Restrict Cloud Provider Region](restrict-region-ui)

  - [Restrict IP Addresses](restrict-ip-addresses-ui)

  - [Restrict cluster Tier Sizes](restrict-cluster-tier-ui)

  - [Require Project Maintenance Windows](require-maintenance-window-ui)

  - [Prevent Modifications to Network Peering](prevent-peering-modifications-ui)

  - [Prevent Modifications to Private Endpoints](prevent-private-endpoint-modifications-ui)

  - [Restrict TLS (Transport Layer Security) Protocol and Cipher Suites](restrict-tls-ui)

  - [Enforce Disk Size (GB)](enforce-disk-size-ui)

  - [Enforce Cluster Topology (Replica Set, Sharded, or Global Cluster)](restrict-cluster-type-ui)

  - [Enforce Minimum Shard Count](restrict-shard-count-ui)

  - [Enforce Dedicated Config Servers for Sharded Clusters](enforce-config-server-management-mode-ui)

  > **Note:** We recommend creating multiple, simple Atlas Resource Policies to make tracking easier.
  > For example, if you want to restrict cloud provider and multiple regions,
  > consider creating one Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the cloud provider and another
  > Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the regions.

sections.heading: Step 3: Click .
sections.body:
This Atlas Resource Policy applies to all new clusters. For existing clusters that
do not comply with the Atlas Resource Policy, Atlas only allows users to make
changes to the cluster which brings the cluster into compliance.
For example, if `cluster0` runs on AWS (Amazon Web Services) and you apply an Atlas Resource Policy that blocks
clusters on AWS (Amazon Web Services), the only change users could make to `cluster0` while
it is non-compliant is switching the cloud provider to Azure (Microsoft Azure) or Google Cloud.To return a list of existing clusters that do not conform to your Atlas Resource Policy,
use the `GET`
[/orgs/\{ORG-ID}/nonCompliantResources](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getorgnoncompliantresources) resource.Atlas generates an [Project Activity Feed](view-activity-feed) when
you create, update, or delete an Atlas Resource Policy. To help identify your activity feed events
and make tracking easier, we recommend creating multiple, simple Atlas Resource Policies.
For example, if you want to restrict the usage of a cloud provider and multiple regions,
consider creating one Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the cloud provider and another
Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the regions.**Tf:**Terraform> **Note:** 
>
> Additional Prerequisites
>
> Complete the following steps before continuing with this guide:
>
> - [Create an API key](atlas/configure-api-access) for your [paying
>   organization](atlas/billing) and store
>   your API keys as environment variables by running
>   the following commands:
>
>   ```bash
>   export MONGODB_ATLAS_PUBLIC_KEY="<your public key>"
>   export MONGODB_ATLAS_PRIVATE_KEY="<your private key>"
>   ```
>
> - Install the [Terraform CLI](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/install)To create an Atlas Resource Policy by using Terraform, configure the [MongoDB Atlas
Terraform Provider](https://registry.terraform.io/providers/mongodb/mongodbatlas/latest/docs),
then construct a policy by using [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/) and specifying it in your
`main.tf` Terraform file.

codeBlocks.content:
export MONGODB_ATLAS_PUBLIC_KEY="<your public key>"
export MONGODB_ATLAS_PRIVATE_KEY="<your private key>"

sections.heading: Step 1: Configure the MongoDB Atlas Terraform Provider.

codeBlocks.content: terraform init -upgrade

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the policy in the Cedar policy language.
sections.body:
Specify the rules for restricting resources using [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/). You can copy
and modify the following example policies for your organization:

- [Restrict Number of Clusters](tf-restrict-number-of-clusters)

- [Restrict Cloud Provider](tf-restrict-cloud-provider)

- [Restrict Region](tf-restrict-region)

- [Restrict Cloud Provider and Region](tf-restrict-provider-region)

- [Restrict IP Addresses](tf-restrict-ip-addresses)

- [Enforce Disk Size (GB)](tf-limit-max-disk-size)

- [Enforce Cluster Topology (Replica Set, Sharded, or Global Cluster)](tf-restrict-cluster-type)

- [Enforce Minimum Shard Count](tf-restrict-shard-count)

- [Enforce Dedicated Config Servers for Sharded Clusters](tf-enforce-config-server-management-mode)

> **Note:** We recommend creating multiple, simple Atlas Resource Policies to make tracking easier.
> For example, if you want to restrict cloud provider and multiple regions,
> consider creating one Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the cloud provider and another
> Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the regions.

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the policies by using Terraform.
sections.body:
After you define the policies you want in your `main.tf` file, run
the following command to create the policies:

```bash
terraform apply
```

codeBlocks.content: terraform apply

sections.heading: Atlas Resource Policy Examples

codeBlocks.content:
forbid (
   principal,
   action == ResourcePolicy::Action::"cluster.modify",
   resource
 )
 unless
 { context.cluster.minTLSVersion == ResourcePolicy::TLSVersion::"tls1_2" };
